name: Flutterwave
what_it_does: Flutterwave is a pan-African payment infrastructure platform supporting cards, mobile money, bank transfers, and USSD across 30+ African currencies via its v3 REST API.
use_cases: Accept payments in local African currencies (NGN, GHS, KES, ZAR, UGX, TZS) without forcing USD conversion
Disburse bulk payouts to mobile money wallets across multiple African countries in a single API call
Create and manage payment plans for subscription billing with stored card authorizations
Collect bank account payments via direct debit in Nigeria and Ghana
Generate hosted payment links for marketplace vendors who need no-code checkout pages
not_for: European or North American domestic payment processing — coverage is Africa-centric
Real-time gross settlement requirements — bank transfers and mobile money have processing delays
Low-latency high-frequency trading or micro-transaction volumes under $0.50
best_when: Building a multi-country African marketplace or remittance product that needs to move money across multiple local payment rails and currencies without forcing currency conversion at the point of sale.
avoid_when: You need a single globally unified payment stack or operate primarily outside Africa.

auth.notes: Secret key passed as Bearer token in Authorization header. Public key used for Inline JS and mobile SDKs. Encryption key required for direct charge endpoints (AES-256-CBC payload encryption).

pricing.paid_tiers: 1.4% for local Mastercard/Visa (Nigeria); international cards at 3.8%; varies by country and payment method
pricing.notes: Pricing varies significantly by country and payment method. Enterprise pricing available for high-volume merchants. No monthly platform fee.

performance.rate_limits: No hard published rate limit; contact support for high-volume needs

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Error responses are inconsistent across payment methods — card errors, mobile money errors, and bank transfer errors use different formats and status codes.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: tx_ref field on charge requests provides reference-based idempotency; duplicate tx_ref values are rejected, but there is no explicit idempotency-key header.
known_agent_gotchas: Direct charge (card-on-file) requires AES-256-CBC payload encryption before sending — not just a Bearer token
Mobile money payment status is eventually consistent; always listen for webhooks rather than trusting the initial charge response
tx_ref must be globally unique per merchant account — using predictable IDs risks collisions across environments
Test mode transactions use a separate set of test card numbers and mobile numbers that differ from Paystack and Stripe test data
Country-specific payment methods (e.g., M-Pesa in Kenya, MTN MoMo in Ghana) require different payload shapes for the same /charges endpoint

security_score_components.security_notes: Requires three separate keys (secret, public, encryption) adding complexity; webhook verification uses a hash computed from tx_ref + amount + currency + secret_key.
